ip_outcome_note: Confirmed Vulpix (Kanto form) via card name, PokemonTCG.io API (swsh2-24, National Pokedex no. 37, Fire type), artist sui, and orange-red multi-tailed fox design. Alolan form (Ice/Fairy, white coat) refuted by coloring.

trivia_bullets.text: First Vulpix print in corpus. National Pokedex no. 37, Fire type, Kanto form. Evolves into Ninetales via Fire Stone. Two forms exist: Kanto (Fire, orange-red) and Alolan (Ice/Fairy, white). Card type indicator and coloring confirm Kanto form.

Flavor text: 'While young, it has six gorgeous tails. When it grows, several new tails are sprouted.' The tail-count-as-age-marker is the core design principle of the line, reflected in the art's multiple-but-compact tail fan.

Vulpix and Ninetales are based on the kyubi no kitsune (nine-tailed fox) of Japanese folklore — supernatural foxes whose power and tail count grow with age. The kitsune's final form (ninth tail, golden transformation) maps onto Ninetales' evolution. Ninetales Pokedex entries warn of a thousand-year curse for grabbing a tail, directly referencing kitsune vengeance mythology.
sources: ScreenRant: 'Pokemon That Are Just Japanese Yokai'; Wikipedia: Ninetales; via WebSearch

trivia_bullets.text: Form disambiguation: Kanto Vulpix (Fire, orange-red coat, warm amber tails) vs. Alolan Vulpix (Ice/Fairy, white coat, pale blue-tinted tails). Both share National Pokedex no. 37 but are treated as distinct forms with separate evolution lines in the games.

related_cards.name: Vulpix (Alolan)
